message.content:
I need to build endpoint-to-scope mappings for 7 project management APIs. For each, I need a JSON file mapping REST API endpoints to OAuth scopes. This is for a proxy-based policy enforcement system.

Output format:
```json
{
  "provider": "notion",
  "apiHost": "api.notion.com",
  "basePath": "/v1",
  "scopeMap": [
    {
      "method": "GET|POST|PATCH|DELETE",
      "pathPattern": "/v1/databases/*",
      "sufficientScopes": ["read_content"],
      "description": "Get a database"
    }
  ],
  "allScopes": ["read_content", ...]
}
```

Use `*` wildcards for variable path segments (IDs).

## 1. Notion (api.notion.com/v1)
Notion uses capability-based scopes. Research the Notion API documentation.
Scopes include: read_content, update_content, insert_content, read_user_information
Endpoints to cover:
- Databases (list, query, get, create, update)
- Pages (get, create, update, archive)
- Blocks (get, list children, append children, update, delete)
- Users (list, get, get me)
- Search
- Comments (list, create)

## 2. Linear (api.linear.app)
Linear uses a GraphQL API at POST /graphql. For REST-like mapping:
- We should map the GraphQL operations to semantic scopes
Linear scopes include: read, write, issues:create, comments:create, admin

Map the main operations:
- Issues (list, get, create, update, delete, archive)
- Projects (list, get, create, update, delete)
- Teams (list, get)
- Users (list, get, me)
- Comments (list, create, update, delete)
- Labels (list, create, update, delete)
- Cycles (list, get, create, update)
- Workflows/states
- Webhooks
Since it's GraphQL, the path will be /graphql for everything, so map by operation type.
For the pathPattern, use POST /graphql and put the GraphQL operation name in the description. For sufficientScopes, map based on whether the operation reads or writes.

## 3. Confluence (your-domain.atlassian.net/wiki/api/v2)
Atlassian Confluence scopes: read:confluence-content.all, read:confluence-content.summary, write:confluence-content, read:confluence-space.summary, write:confluence-space, read:confluence-props, write:confluence-props, read:confluence-user, write:confluence-user, search:confluence

Endpoints:
- Spaces (list, get, create, update, delete)
- Pages (list, get, create, update, delete)
- Blog posts (list, get, create, update, delete)
- Comments (list, get, create, update, delete)
- Attachments
- Labels
- Search
- Users

Base path: /wiki/api/v2

## 4. Asana (app.asana.com/api/1.0)
Asana scopes are broader: default (full access). But for our semantic mapping, define:
- tasks.read, tasks.write, projects.read, projects.write, workspaces.read, workspaces.write, users.read, teams.read, tags.read, tags.write, sections.read, sections.write, goals.read, goals.write, portfolios.read, portfolios.write

Endpoints:
- Tasks (list, get, create, update, delete, subtasks, dependencies, stories)
- Projects (list, get, create, update, delete, sections, task lists)
- Workspaces (list, get, update)
- Users (list, get, me)
- Teams (list, get)
- Tags (list, get, create, update, delete)
- Sections (list, get, create, update, delete)
- Custom fields
- Portfolios
- Goals
- Status updates
- Webhooks

Base path: /api/1.0

## 5. Monday.com (api.monday.com/v2)
Monday.com uses GraphQL. Scopes: me:read, boards:read, boards:write, workspaces:read, workspaces:write, users:read, users:write, account:read, notifications:write, tags:read, teams:read, updates:read, updates:write, assets:read

Map operations:
- Boards (list, get, create, update, delete, archive)
- Items (list, get, create, update, delete, move)
- Columns
- Groups
- Updates/comments
- Users
- Workspaces
- Tags
- Webhooks/subscriptions

Since GraphQL, path is POST /v2 for all. Map by operation in description.

## 6. ClickUp (api.clickup.com/api/v2)
ClickUp scopes are limited — typically full access. Define semantic scopes:
- tasks.read, tasks.write, spaces.read, spaces.write, lists.read, lists.write, folders.read, folders.write, teams.read, goals.read, goals.write, comments.read, comments.write, time_tracking.read, time_tracking.write, webhooks.write

Endpoints:
- Tasks (get, create, update, delete)
- Lists (get, create, update, delete)
- Folders (get, create, update, delete)
- Spaces (get, create, update, delete)
- Teams/workspaces (get)
- Goals (get, create, update, delete)
- Comments (get, create, update, delete)
- Time tracking entries
- Tags
- Custom fields
- Views
- Webhooks

Base path: /api/v2

## 7. Trello (api.trello.com/1)
Trello scopes: read, write, account
Endpoints:
- Boards (list, get, create, update, delete, lists, cards, members)
- Lists (get, create, update, archive, cards)
- Cards (get, create, update, delete, comments, attachments, checklists, labels, members)
- Members (get, boards)
- Organizations (list, get, boards, members)
- Actions/activity
- Labels (get, create, update, delete)
- Checklists (get, create, update, delete, check items)
- Webhooks
- Search
- Notifications

Base path: /1

Write these files:
- /workspace/scope-maps/notion.json
- /workspace/scope-maps/linear.json
- /workspace/scope-maps/confluence.json
- /workspace/scope-maps/asana.json
- /workspace/scope-maps/monday.json
- /workspace/scope-maps/clickup.json
- /workspace/scope-maps/trello.json

Search the web for each provider's API reference documentation to get accurate endpoint paths and scope requirements. Be thorough — include all major endpoints.

Here's what I found about the Asana REST API endpoints reference documentation:

## Asana REST API Reference Documentation

### Official API Reference

The Asana REST API reference is generated from their OpenAPI Specification and contains a comprehensive reference for objects, schemas, and endpoints available in the API. You can access it at: **[developers.asana.com/reference/rest-api-reference](https://developers.asana.com/reference/rest-api-reference)**

### Key Features

- **API Explorer**: Asana's REST API reference allows you to explore different REST API endpoints through an in-context, easy-to-use interface. Using this built-in API explorer, you can make API requests (and view responses) directly in your browser.

- **Authentication**: Before using the API explorer, be sure to create a personal access token (if you do not already have one). Generating a PAT allows you to access (i.e., make requests to) the Asana REST API upon successful authentication.

- **Base URL**: API endpoints are relative to the following base URL: `https://app.asana.com/api/1.0`.

### Documentation Structure

Documentation on the site is divided into two major sections: **Guides** (contextual information, guides, and tutorials regarding API usage, including how to work with opt fields, pagination, handling errors, rate limits, working with custom fields, storing external data, working with rich text, SCIM, Audit log events, and deprecations) and **API reference** (a comprehensive reference for objects, schemas, and endpoints available in the API).

### API Characteristics

- The Asana API is a RESTful interface with predictable resource-oriented URLs, accepts JSON or form-encoded requests, returns JSON, and uses standard HTTP features (verbs, response codes, etc.).

- The Asana API accepts JSON or form-encoded content in requests and returns JSON content in all of its responses, including errors. If the API sees that no form fields are present, it attempts to look for a JSON body.

### Getting Started

- Visit the API Reference and learn about the different types of APIs that are available. Their embedded API explorer lets you try using the API from the reference. You can build your request on the documentation page, press "Try it" and see a response in seconds.

- If you would like to make requests against data outside of your Asana instance (e.g., for testing purposes), they recommend setting up a developer sandbox.

### Useful Links

| API Reference | [developers.asana.com/reference/rest-api-reference](https://developers.asana.com/reference/rest-api-reference) |
| Developer Docs Home | [developers.asana.com](https://developers.asana.com/) |
| Quick Start Guide | [developers.asana.com/docs/quick-start](https://developers.asana.com/docs/quick-start) |

| Asana Developers Program | [asana.com/developers](https://asana.com/developers) |

Here's a summary of what I found about the **ClickUp API v2 REST Endpoints Reference Documentation**:

## Official ClickUp API Developer Resources

The primary source for ClickUp API documentation is the **official ClickUp Developer Portal**:

- **Developer Hub**: [developer.clickup.com](https://developer.clickup.com/) — ClickUp offers a developer-first API to connect external systems, streamline workflows, and build tailored solutions.

- **Getting Started Guide**: [developer.clickup.com/docs/Getting Started](https://developer.clickup.com/docs/Getting%20Started) — In the API reference section you will find a few individual endpoints that are defined as API v3. ClickUp is working on updating the rest of the API to v3 and providing a dedicated guide for API v3. In the meantime, developers should familiarize themselves with the differences between API v2 and v3.

- **API Reference (e.g., Get Tasks)**: [developer.clickup.com/reference/gettasks](https://developer.clickup.com/reference/gettasks) — You can only view task information of tasks you can access. This endpoint only includes tasks where the specified `list_id` is their home List. Tasks added to the `list_id` with a different home List are not included in the response by default.

## Key Details About the ClickUp API v2

### API Basics
- The ClickUp API is a REST API that uses predictable URLs and common HTTP response codes, and returns data in JSON format.
- The API exposes "resources" that you can create, read, update, and delete (CRUD).
- The API provides a range of endpoints for different resources. For example, you can create, update, or retrieve tasks using the `/task` endpoint. Data is returned in JSON format.

### Authentication
- To use the ClickUp API, you must authenticate every request. For personal use, authenticate with your personal API token. For apps or integrations that other people use, use the OAuth flow, allowing users to authorize Workspaces for your app. Include the token in the `Authorization` header of your requests.
- The ClickUp API enforces request rate limits per token, and rate limits vary depending on your Workspace Plan.

### API v2 vs. v3 Terminology
- In the `/api/v3/` endpoints, Workspaces are used consistently to refer to the main organizational unit, replacing the term "Team" from earlier versions. The transition from "Teams" to "Workspaces" in v3 reflects the current naming convention within ClickUp.
- This removes the ambiguity that existed in v2 where "Team" was used in the same context.

### Additional Resources
- **GitHub Demo Repo**: [github.com/clickup/clickup-APIv2-demo](https://github.com/clickup/clickup-APIv2-demo) — This repository contains a demo application showcasing a ClickUp to ClickUp integration, demonstrating basic REST functionality, webhook handling, and OAuth implementation using the ClickUp API v2.
- **Postman Collection**: [Postman - ClickUp API V2 Release 2.127](https://www.postman.com/cloudy-meadow-6388/okk/documentation/m5wbnsb/clickup-api-v2-release-2-127) — A Postman documentation collection for ClickUp API V2.
- **ClickUp Help Center (API Section)**: [help.clickup.com](https://help.clickup.com/hc/en-us/sections/6131847925911-API)

The most comprehensive and up-to-date reference for all ClickUp API v2 REST endpoints is the official developer portal at **[developer.clickup.com](https://developer.clickup.com/)**, where you can browse individual endpoint references for tasks, lists, folders, spaces, teams, webhooks, and more.

Here's a comprehensive overview of Linear's API GraphQL OAuth scopes based on the reference documentation:

## Linear API – OAuth Scopes Reference

### Authentication Overview

The Linear API supports personal API keys and OAuth2 authentication. If you're building an application for others to use, they recommend you use OAuth2 authentication.

### Available OAuth Scopes

Linear OAuth supports the following scopes:
- **`read`** (default): Read access for the user's account
- **`write`**: Write access for the user's account
- **`issues:create`**: Allows creating new issues and their attachments
- **`comments:create`**: Allows creating new issue comments
- **`timeSchedule:write`**: Allows creating and modifying time schedules
- **`admin`**: Full access to admin level endpoints

The `read` scope must always be included.

### Additional Scopes (Recently Added)

Linear added the ability to use the `initiative:read` and `initiative:write` scopes when initiating OAuth with `actor=app` to give the app user access to workspace-level initiative entities. You can also now use the `customer:read` or `customer:write` scopes when initiating OAuth with `actor=app` to give the app user access to workspace-level customer entities (Customer, CustomerStatus, and CustomerTier).

### OAuth Authorization Flow

When authorizing a user to the Linear API, redirect to an authorization URL with correct parameters and scopes. The authorization URL format is:

```
GET https://linear.app/oauth/authorize?response_type=code&client_id=YOUR_CLIENT_ID&redirect_uri=YOUR_REDIRECT_URL&state=SECURE_RANDOM&scope=read
```

Linear supports the PKCE flow.

### Refresh Tokens

OAuth2 applications created from October 1, 2025 onwards have refresh tokens enabled by default for user-initiated OAuth with no option to disable them. OAuth2 applications created before then have until April 1, 2026 to migrate to the new refresh token system.

The access token is valid for 24 hours and will need to be refreshed when it expires.

### Client Credentials Grant

Linear supports the `client_credentials` grant type for OAuth2 apps that use tokens for server-to-server communication and cannot support a user-initiated OAuth flow involving refresh tokens. The token generated using this grant type will be an app actor token that has access to all public teams in the workspace and is valid for 30 days.

Every OAuth2 app can only have one active client credentials token at a time. If you request a new client credentials token while you still have an active one, it will invalidate the currently active token and return a new one with a 30-day validity.

### Scope Upgrades

Linear has improved support for upgrading scopes for an existing app token during the authorization code flow without revoking and regenerating the token. Additionally, in workspaces with OAuth app approvals, users can now request approval for additional scopes beyond the initial set of scopes an app was approved for.

### Rate Limits

For OAuth app authentication, the rate limit is 500 requests per hour per user/app, with 200,000 complexity points per hour per user/app.

### Key Resources

- **Developer Documentation**: [developers.linear.app](https://developers.linear.app)
- **OAuth Authentication Guide**: [linear.app/developers/oauth-2-0-authentication](https://linear.app/developers/oauth-2-0-authentication)
- **GraphQL Schema Explorer**: [Apollo Studio - Linear API](https://studio.apollographql.com/public/Linear-API/schema/reference?variant=current)

## Notion API Authentication & OAuth

Public integrations follow the OAuth 2.0 protocol, which allows workspace members to give access to Notion pages directly through the auth flow, without having to open each Notion workspace page directly and manually give permission to the integration.

An internal integration allows Notion workspace members to interact with the workspace through the Notion REST API. Each internal integration is tied to a single, specific workspace and only members within the workspace can use the integration. After an internal integration is added to a workspace, members must manually give the integration access to the specific pages or databases that they want it to use.

## Bearer Token Authentication

The Notion API accepts bearer tokens in the `Authorization` header. Bearer tokens are provided to you when you create an integration. If you're creating a public OAuth integration, the integration also receives bearer tokens each time a user completes the OAuth flow.

## Latest API Version

You should call the API with required headers: `Authorization: Bearer ...`, `Notion-Version: 2025-09-03`, and `Content-Type: application/json` where needed. Notion's versioning documentation states versioning is required via the `Notion-Version` header, and it gives **2025-09-03** as the latest version at time of writing.

## OAuth Scopes & Permissions

The Notion API uses OAuth for authentication. Permissions are managed through scopes, allowing fine-grained control over what the API can access and manipulate.

Integration capabilities (scopes/permissions) determine what types of objects/actions the integration is allowed to request. Additionally, a shared content boundary defines which pages/databases a user has explicitly shared with the integration in the Notion UI.

## API Endpoints

The Notion API provides a variety of endpoints for interacting with content, including: **Pages** (create, retrieve, update, and delete pages), **Databases** (manage databases and their schemas), and **Blocks** (manipulate individual blocks within pages).

To add a simple paragraph, for instance, you'd send a request to the `v1/blocks/{block_id}/children` endpoint. The body of that request would contain a JSON object defining a paragraph block with your text inside. This block-based system is what lets you programmatically build beautifully formatted pages.

## Public vs. Internal Integrations

Notion distinguishes between internal integrations (used within a single workspace) and public integrations (distributed to many workspaces). The authorization process differs: internal integrations typically use a static token created in the integration settings, while public integrations use OAuth so each user/workspace grants access and your app receives tokens per installation.

## Setting Up OAuth for Notion

You need to select "Public Integration" on the configuration page to get an `oauth_id` and `oauth_secret`. Private integrations do not provide these details.

Make sure your Notion integration has the appropriate capabilities enabled. For user authentication, you'll need the "Read user information including email addresses" capability.

## Best Practices

Always pin a version and set `Notion-Version` explicitly in your HTTP client. Tokens should be treated like passwords — do not commit them to Git, do not place them in client-side JavaScript, and never ship them to end users. Use environment variables and a secure secret manager for production.

---

Here's a summary of what I found about Confluence Cloud REST API v2 endpoints, OAuth scopes, and related 2025 updates:

## Confluence Cloud REST API v2 — OAuth Scopes Overview

### API v2 and Granular OAuth 2.0 Scopes

The Confluence Cloud REST API v2 is designed with definitions and performance intended to be an improvement over v1. A key design principle is that the separation of endpoints allows REST API V2 to be fully compatible with granular OAuth 2.0 scopes.

Endpoints that have specific, restricted scope behave predictably, are easier to optimize, and are compatible with Atlassian's commitment to granular OAuth 2.0 scopes.

### Authentication & Authorization

If you are building a Cloud app, authentication is implemented via JWT or OAuth 2.0, depending on what you're building. Authorization can be implemented by scopes or by OAuth 2.0 user impersonation.

### Granular Scopes per Endpoint

For example, the **Pages** endpoints use specific granular scopes:
- The OAuth 2.0 scope required for reading pages is `read:page:confluence`, with the Connect app scope being `READ`.

### No Expansions in v2

REST API V2 does not support expansions or the `expand` query parameter, as expansions are contrary to the concept of specialized endpoints since they enable fetching arbitrary data types from a single endpoint.

### Recent 2025 Changelog Updates

Several important scope additions have been announced:

1. **Folders** — Endpoints and scopes have been added for Confluence Folders, with three new scopes for Folders: read, write, and delete, supporting the implementation of Folders in Forge applications, along with creation, retrieval, and deletion of Folders via v2 REST APIs.

2. **Smart Links & Databases** — Endpoints and scopes have been added for Confluence Smart Links in the content tree and Databases, with three new scopes (read, write, and delete) for each, supporting the implementation of Smart Links and Databases in Forge applications.

3. **App Activity Logs** — App activity logs came to Atlassian audit logs at the end of February 2025 for admins with Atlassian Guard Premium or Cloud Enterprise licenses, recording activity by third-party apps in the form of API calls to Jira and Confluence.

4. **Rate Limiting** — All Forge, Connect, and OAuth 2.0 (3LO) apps are in scope for rate limiting enforcement, though API token-based traffic is not affected, and the vast majority of apps are already operating well within these limits.

### How to Use OAuth 2.0 (3LO) with the API

Requests that use OAuth 2.0 (3LO) are made via `api.atlassian.com`, not `https://your-domain.atlassian.net`. The request URL structure for Confluence apps is: `https://api.atlassian.com/ex/confluence/{cloudid}/{api}`.

### Key Resources

- **Scopes reference**: [Confluence scopes for OAuth 2.0 (3LO) and Forge apps](https://developer.atlassian.com/cloud/confluence/scopes-for-oauth-2-3LO-and-forge-apps/)
- **REST API v2 reference**: [Confluence Cloud REST API v2](https://developer.atlassian.com/cloud/confluence/rest/v2/intro/)
- **OAuth 2.0 changelog**: [OAuth 2.0 changelog](https://developer.atlassian.com/cloud/oauth/changelog/)
- **Confluence Cloud changelog**: [Confluence Cloud changelog](https://developer.atlassian.com/cloud/confluence/changelog/)

The Trello API supports basic OAuth 1.0, using the following URLs:
- `https://trello.com/1/OAuthGetRequestToken`
- `https://trello.com/1/OAuthAuthorizeToken`
- `https://trello.com/1/OAuthGetAccessToken`

Your application secret is listed in the API Key tab on your Power-Up management page at `https://trello.com/power-ups/admin`.

### OAuth Scopes

Trello supports the following scopes when requesting authorization:

- **`read`** – Read-only access to boards, cards, members, etc.
- **`write`** – Read and write access.
- **`account`** – Member emails can only be accessed when the `account` scope is requested. Once granted, the token generated can only be used to access the email address of the user who granted access.

Scopes can be combined, for example: `scope=read,write`.

### Generating Tokens

If you're just getting started, you can generate a token for yourself using your API key and a URL like: `https://trello.com/1/authorize?expiration=1day&scope=read&response_type=token&key={YourAPIKey}`

### Passing Authorization Credentials

The easiest and quickest way to pass authorization to Trello is via query parameters.

The Trello API will also accept authorization via an Authorization header with the format: `OAuth oauth_consumer_key="{{apiKey}}", oauth_token="{{apiToken}}"`.

### Token Security

Tokens for users should always be securely stored as they grant access to the entire user's account. It is ok for your API key to be publicly available, but a token should never be publicly available. If a token becomes public, it should be revoked immediately by the user.

### Managing Authorized Apps

Trello users can view metadata regarding authorized applications by visiting their account settings page. Here's a comprehensive overview of Monday.com's API v2, GraphQL operations, and OAuth scopes:

## Monday.com API v2 — GraphQL & OAuth Scopes

### GraphQL API Overview

Monday.com's API is an application layer that allows apps to read and update data inside a monday.com account, supporting operations on boards, items, column values, users, workspaces, and more.

Unlike REST APIs, where multiple endpoints return different data, GraphQL always exposes one endpoint. Monday.com's API uses the following endpoint: `https://api.monday.com/v2`.

### GraphQL Operations

There are two possible operations in GraphQL: **queries** and **mutations**. Queries perform the READ operation and do not change or alter any data. The result of each query will be formatted in the same way as the query itself.

The GraphQL schema defines the structure of the available API data and contains all of the available queries and mutations. It is a valuable resource for verifying API responses and extracting useful information from the metadata. Monday.com exposes their schema publicly.

### Authentication

The monday.com platform API utilizes personal V2 API tokens to authenticate requests and identify the user making the call. These tokens are unique to each user and have no explicit length.

Their permissions mirror what you can do in the monday.com UI, ensuring that API access is consistent with your platform-level permissions. Personal tokens mirror all permission levels set in the monday.com UI, including board, column, item, or account access. If you don't have permission to access a certain workspace via the UI, you won't have permission using your personal API token either.

App tokens have an additional set of permission scopes that specify which queries and mutations they can access, while personal tokens have all permission scopes.

### OAuth 2.0 & Permission Scopes

OAuth 2.0 is a protocol that lets your app request authorization to read or modify data in a user's monday account.

At the end of the OAuth process, your app gets an access token that belongs to the user and grants access to specified permission scopes.

OAuth scopes define what your app can and cannot do. Each endpoint in the monday API requires a certain permission scope that can be found in their API reference.

#### Available OAuth Scopes

Monday.com requires you to specify scopes for API access. Common scopes include:

- `account:read`
- `assets:read`
- `boards:read`
- `boards:write`
- `docs:read`
- `docs:write`
- `me:read`
- `notifications:write`
- `tags:read`
- `teams:read`
- `teams:write`
- `updates:read`
- `updates:write`
- `users:read`
- `users:write`
- `webhooks:read`
- `webhooks:write`
- `workspaces:read`
- `workspaces:write`

These scopes define the permissions your app can request during the OAuth authorization process.

It's essential to select only the scopes necessary for your application's functionality to adhere to the principle of least privilege.

### OAuth Flow

A successful OAuth authorization involves making an authorization request and redirecting the user to the monday OAuth URL with the client ID: `https://auth.monday.com/oauth2/authorize`. The user sees the permission scopes your app is requesting and approves the request.

Once you have obtained an authorization code from the authorization endpoint, you can exchange it for an access token to make calls to monday's API. The authorization code is only valid for 10 minutes.

The token URL is `https://auth.monday.com/oauth2/token` (POST request).

### 2025 API Version

Monday offers a GraphQL-based API, and the latest reference was built using the Monday API Reference Version 2025-04. Updated API version configurations now use version 2025-07 with improved functionality and support for inline data sources and GraphQL fragment support for advanced queries.

Here's a comprehensive overview of the **Notion API capabilities and scopes** based on the search results:

## Notion API Integration Capabilities

The Notion API uses a **capabilities-based permission system** that determines what an integration can do within a workspace. These capabilities are set during the integration setup process.

### Content Capabilities

There are three main content capabilities:

1. **Read Content**: This capability gives an integration access to read existing content in a Notion workspace. For example, an integration with only this capability is able to call "Retrieve a database," but not "Update database."

2. **Update Content**: Update existing content within a database and pages. The Read Content capability alone does not give the integration access to read full objects. For example, an integration with only this capability is able to Create a page but is not able to update existing pages.

3. **Insert Content**: If your integration is solely bringing data into Notion (creating new pages, or adding blocks), your integration only needs Insert content capabilities.

It is possible for an integration to have any combination of these content capabilities.

### Comment Capabilities

Comment capabilities dictate how an integration can interact with the comments on a page or block.

- **Read Comments**: This capability gives the integration permission to read comments from a Notion page or block.
- **Insert Comments**: This capability gives the integration permission to insert comments in a page or in an existing discussion.

### User Capabilities

An integration can request different levels of user capabilities, which affect how user objects are returned from the Notion API: "No user information" prevents an integration from requesting any information about users. Other options include user information without email addresses, and full access to user information including name, profile image, and email address.

### Key Principles

- **Capabilities never supersede user permissions**: An integration's capabilities will never supersede a user's. If a user loses edit access to the page where they have added an integration, that integration will now also only have read access, regardless of the capabilities the integration was created with.

- **Re-authentication for public integrations**: For public integrations, users will need to re-authenticate with an integration if the capabilities are changed in the time since the user last authenticated with the integration.

- **Principle of least privilege**: In general, you want to request minimum capabilities that your integration needs in order to function. The fewer capabilities you request, the more likely a workspace admin will be able to install your integration.

### Setting Up Capabilities

When creating an integration, you configure capabilities in the **Capabilities tab**. For example, as described in the n8n documentation: Open the Capabilities tab. Select these capabilities: Read content, Update content, Insert content, User information without email addresses. Select Save changes.

### Authentication

The Notion API uses OAuth for authentication. Permissions are managed through scopes, allowing fine-grained control over what the API can access and manipulate.

### Data Models Supported

The Notion API provides a variety of endpoints for interacting with content, including: Pages (create, retrieve, update, and delete pages), Databases (manage databases and their schemas), and Blocks (manipulate individual blocks within pages).

For the official documentation, refer to the [Notion Integration Capabilities reference](https://developers.notion.com/reference/capabilities).

### Key Resources & Endpoints

1. **Tasks** – Tasks are the basic unit of action in Asana. Tasks have many fields, including a single assignee, name, notes, followers (i.e., collaborators), likes, and comments (among others). Tasks inherit custom fields from their parent project(s), and custom field values are set for each individual task.

2. **Projects** – A project represents a prioritized list of tasks in Asana or a board with columns of tasks represented as cards. A project exists in a single workspace or organization and is accessible to a subset of users in that workspace or organization, depending on its permissions.

3. **Workspaces** – A workspace is the highest-level organizational unit in Asana. A workspace exists as the highest level of organization to organize teams.

4. **Sections** – A section is a group of tasks within a project. Sections let you divide tasks into categories, workflow stages, priorities, and more.

5. **Portfolios** – Custom fields can be added to portfolios in addition to standard fields that are displayed on every portfolio. These fields provide a high-level overview of the status of each project within the portfolio.

6. **Goals** – You can view endpoints like GET /goals in the API reference, and review the enumerated values listed in the opt_fields section.

7. **Tags** – The GET /projects, GET /users, and GET /tags endpoints will soon require a workspace or team parameter when making requests for users who belong to multiple workspaces. Cross-workspace fetching will be deprecated.

### Webhooks
Asana webhooks are HTTP callbacks that deliver instant notifications when tasks, projects, stories, and other resources change within your Asana workspace. Unlike traditional API polling which repeatedly queries for updates, webhooks "push" event data to your server immediately after changes occur, reducing latency and API quota consumption.

- **Limits**: Asana has two webhook limits: (1) 10,000 webhooks per access token, and (2) 1,000 webhooks per resource.
- **Filters**: Filters support resource_type (e.g., "task", "story"), resource_subtype (e.g., "milestone"), action (e.g., "changed", "added"), and fields (specific field changes when action is "changed").
- **Heartbeats**: Asana sends periodic heartbeat events to your webhook endpoint every 8 hours to keep track of the last time that delivery succeeded. If no response to heartbeat events is received after 24 hours, the registered webhook connection will be deleted.

### Authentication
The easiest way to get started is to create a personal access token (which is like a traditional "API key" for your user). Beyond that, there are several options for authenticating, such as a service account and OAuth (including OpenID Connect). The purpose of your app, who uses it, and its features will dictate the method of auth that's best for your app.

### Object Hierarchy
Objects in the API (e.g., tasks, projects, portfolios, etc.) can also be associated with one or more other objects. For instance, a story may be associated with a task. This task may belong to one (or more) projects. That project (along with other related projects) may be shared with a particular team. And at the top, a workspace exists as the highest level of organization.

### Key Reference Links
- **API Reference**: https://developers.asana.com/reference/rest-api-reference
- **Tasks**: https://developers.asana.com/reference/tasks
- **Projects**: https://developers.asana.com/reference/projects
- **FAQ**: https://developers.asana.com/docs/faq

Here's a comprehensive overview of the **Monday.com GraphQL API** covering queries, mutations, boards, items, columns, groups, users, and workspaces:

## Monday.com GraphQL API Overview

The monday GraphQL API is part of the monday apps framework. It is an application layer that allows apps to read and update data inside a monday.com account. It supports operations on boards, items, column values, users, workspaces, and more.

The monday.com API is built with GraphQL, a flexible query language that allows you to return as much or as little data as you need. GraphQL is an application layer that parses the query you send it and returns (or changes) data accordingly.

---

### Queries vs. Mutations

There are two possible operations in GraphQL: queries and mutations. Queries perform the READ operation and do not change or alter any data. The result of each query will be formatted in the same way as the query itself.

Mutations are special queries that perform the CUD (Create, Update, Delete) operations to modify your data. Mutations return an instance of the object you just modified, so you can query the data you changed.

---

### Request Structure

All requests to the API must be POST requests. Queries must conform to the GraphQL schema. Authentication: all requests to the API should include an authentication token in the request header.

All requests to the API should have a JSON-formatted body. The only exception is file uploads, which should be multipart requests.

---

### Boards

The monday.com API can retrieve objects and their associated fields from the Work OS. You can retrieve a list of boards along with their names and board IDs by specifying the "boards" object in your query and the number of results you'd like to return.

You can also create boards with mutations. When creating a new board via the API, you can now set the item nickname configuration for items on a board using the `item_nickname` argument.

---

### Items

You can run a GraphQL `items_page` query to retrieve the first 500 items from a board. This query returns a cursor value and the first 500 items on your board.

Using that cursor value as an argument, run a `next_items_page` query to fetch the next relevant set of items. Each cursor is valid for 60 minutes after the initial request.

To create items, you can use the `create_item` mutation with variables:
Variables are passed as a JSON object, and GraphQL will dynamically insert them into the query. You need to declare the type of the variables in your query and pass them as a separate JSON object inside the API request.

---

### Columns

You can retrieve the ID, title, and type of each column on a board.

The GraphQL schema defines a set of column values as a JSON string (key-value pairs). The keys of the `column_values` object must be column IDs, and the values must be structured depending on the type of the column.

You can specify a column's width using the `update_column` mutation (API version 2026-01).

Common column types include: status, text, numbers, date, people, dropdown, checkbox, email, phone, link, timeline, tags, and rating.

---

### Groups

Groups can be managed via the API. You can create new groups on a board using the `create_group` mutation with a `board_id` and `group_name`, and items can be assigned to specific groups when created using the `group_id` argument.

---

### Users & Workspaces

You can query workspaces using membership kind filters (e.g., `member`) and retrieve fields like id, name, kind, and description. Use `all` to return all workspaces the user has access to, or `member` to return only the workspaces the user is explicitly a member of.

---

### Recent API Updates (Late 2025 / Early 2026)

- You can now retrieve an account's creation date using the new `created_at` field on account queries (API version 2026-01).
- A new `create_project` mutation allows creating projects with parameters like name, board_kind, workspace_id, folder_id, and companions.
- Enterprise customers can now manage departments via the API, with new queries and mutations for creating, updating, assigning, and deleting departments.

---

### Schema & Documentation

The GraphQL schema defines the structure of the available API data and contains all of the available queries and mutations. It is a valuable resource for verifying API responses and extracting useful information from the metadata. The schema is exposed in introspection JSON format by default for the current API version.

You can request a different version using the optional `version=<API-Version>` parameter, or retrieve the SDL version using the `format=sdl` parameter.

For full details, visit the official docs at [developer.monday.com](https://developer.monday.com/api-reference/docs/basics).

Here's a comprehensive overview of the **ClickUp API v2 endpoints**, organized by resource category. The base URL for all endpoints is `https://api.clickup.com/api/v2/`.

---

## ClickUp API v2 — Endpoint Reference

The ClickUp API is a RESTful API that allows developers to interact with various resources within the ClickUp platform, including tasks, lists, folders, spaces, and more. ClickUp's hierarchy is: Workspace → Spaces → Folders → Lists → Tasks → Subtasks.

> **Important terminology note:** In the /api/v2/ endpoints, "Teams" refer to what ClickUp now more commonly calls Workspaces. In API v2, Teams are the overarching organizational entity that contains users, Spaces, Folders, Lists, and tasks. Think of a Team in v2 as equivalent to a Workspace.

---

### 🔐 Authorization / OAuth
| Method | Path |
|--------|------|
| POST | `/api/v2/oauth/token` |

The `/api/v2/oauth/token` endpoint is used for authing the API and going through the OAuth flow. Applications utilizing a personal API token don't use this endpoint.

---

### 👤 User
| Method | Path |
|--------|------|
| GET | `/api/v2/user` |

You should call `GET /api/v2/user` before running any automation to confirm the token is valid.

---

### 🏢 Teams (Workspaces)
| Method | Path |
|--------|------|
| GET | `/api/v2/team` |

---

### 🌐 Spaces
| Method | Path |
|--------|------|
| GET | `/api/v2/team/{team_id}/space` |
| POST | `/api/v2/team/{team_id}/space` |
| GET | `/api/v2/space/{space_id}` |
| PUT | `/api/v2/space/{space_id}` |
| DELETE | `/api/v2/space/{space_id}` |

---

### 📁 Folders
| Method | Path |
|--------|------|
| GET | `/api/v2/space/{space_id}/folder` |
| POST | `/api/v2/space/{space_id}/folder` |
| GET | `/api/v2/folder/{folder_id}` |
| PUT | `/api/v2/folder/{folder_id}` |
| DELETE | `/api/v2/folder/{folder_id}` |

---

### 📋 Lists
| Method | Path |
|--------|------|
| GET | `/api/v2/folder/{folder_id}/list` |
| POST | `/api/v2/folder/{folder_id}/list` |
| GET | `/api/v2/space/{space_id}/list` (folderless lists) |
| POST | `/api/v2/space/{space_id}/list` (folderless lists) |
| GET | `/api/v2/list/{list_id}` |
| PUT | `/api/v2/list/{list_id}` |
| DELETE | `/api/v2/list/{list_id}` |

The Get Lists endpoint uses `https://api.clickup.com/api/v2/folder/{folder_id}/list` to view the Lists within a Folder.

---

### ✅ Tasks
| Method | Path |
|--------|------|
| GET | `/api/v2/list/{list_id}/task` |
| POST | `/api/v2/list/{list_id}/task` |
| GET | `/api/v2/task/{task_id}` |
| PUT | `/api/v2/task/{task_id}` |
| DELETE | `/api/v2/task/{task_id}` |
| GET | `/api/v2/team/{team_id}/task` (filtered team tasks) |
| POST | `/api/v2/list/{list_id}/task/{task_id}` (add task to list) |
| DELETE | `/api/v2/list/{list_id}/task/{task_id}` (remove task from list) |

The Get Tasks endpoint at `https://api.clickup.com/api/v2/list/{list_id}/task` views the tasks in a List, with responses limited to 100 tasks per page.

The Add Task To List endpoint at `/api/v2/list/{list_id}/task/{task_id}` adds a task to an additional List. This endpoint requires the Tasks in Multiple List ClickApp to be enabled.

---

### 💬 Comments
| Method | Path |
|--------|------|
| GET | `/api/v2/task/{task_id}/comment` |
| POST | `/api/v2/task/{task_id}/comment` |
| GET | `/api/v2/list/{list_id}/comment` |
| POST | `/api/v2/list/{list_id}/comment` |
| GET | `/api/v2/view/{view_id}/comment` (chat view) |
| POST | `/api/v2/view/{view_id}/comment` (chat view) |
| PUT | `/api/v2/comment/{comment_id}` |
| DELETE | `/api/v2/comment/{comment_id}` |

The ClickUp API allows you to view, add, and update or delete comments on tasks, Lists, and Chat views.

---

### 👥 Members
| Method | Path |
|--------|------|
| GET | `/api/v2/task/{task_id}/member` |
| GET | `/api/v2/list/{list_id}/member` |

To get a list of team members that have access to a particular task or list, use the routes `GET /api/v2/task/{{task_id}}/member` and `GET /api/v2/list/{{list_id}}/member`.

---

### 🎯 Goals
| Method | Path |
|--------|------|
| GET | `/api/v2/team/{team_id}/goal` |
| POST | `/api/v2/team/{team_id}/goal` |
| GET | `/api/v2/goal/{goal_id}` |
| PUT | `/api/v2/goal/{goal_id}` |
| DELETE | `/api/v2/goal/{goal_id}` |
| POST | `/api/v2/goal/{goal_id}/key_result` |
| PUT | `/api/v2/key_result/{key_result_id}` |
| DELETE | `/api/v2/key_result/{key_result_id}` |

API webhooks can broadly track all create, update, delete events for tasks, Lists, Folders, Spaces, Goals, and Targets.

---

### 🔔 Webhooks
| Method | Path |
|--------|------|
| GET | `/api/v2/team/{team_id}/webhook` |
| POST | `/api/v2/team/{team_id}/webhook` |
| PUT | `/api/v2/webhook/{webhook_id}` |
| DELETE | `/api/v2/webhook/{webhook_id}` |

The Get Webhooks endpoint at `https://api.clickup.com/api/v2/team/{team_id}/webhook` views the webhooks created via the API for a Workspace. This endpoint returns webhooks created by the authenticated user.

Webhook events include: taskCreated (triggered when a new task is created), taskUpdated (triggered when a task is updated), and many more.

---

### ⏱ Time Tracking (Legacy)
| Method | Path |
|--------|------|
| POST | `/api/v2/task/{task_id}/time` |
| GET | `/api/v2/task/{task_id}/time` |
| DELETE | `/api/v2/task/{task_id}/time/{interval_id}` |
| PUT | `/api/v2/task/{task_id}/time/{interval_id}` |

The `/api/v2/task/{task_id}/time` endpoint is a legacy time tracking endpoint. ClickUp recommends using the Time Tracking API endpoints to manage time entries.

---

### 📎 Attachments
| Method | Path |
|--------|------|
| POST | `/api/v2/task/{task_id}/attachment` |

---

### 🏷 Tags
| Method | Path |
|--------|------|
| GET | `/api/v2/space/{space_id}/tag` |
| POST | `/api/v2/space/{space_id}/tag` |
| PUT | `/api/v2/space/{space_id}/tag/{tag_name}` |
| DELETE | `/api/v2/space/{space_id}/tag/{tag_name}` |
| POST | `/api/v2/task/{task_id}/tag/{tag_name}` |
| DELETE | `/api/v2/task/{task_id}/tag/{tag_name}` |

---

### 🔧 Additional Endpoint Categories
The API also includes endpoints for:
- **Custom Fields** — `/api/v2/list/{list_id}/field`, `/api/v2/task/{task_id}/field/{field_id}`
- **Views** — `/api/v2/team/{team_id}/view`, `/api/v2/space/{space_id}/view`, `/api/v2/folder/{folder_id}/view`, `/api/v2/list/{list_id}/view`
- **Dependencies** — `/api/v2/task/{task_id}/dependency`
- **Checklists** — `/api/v2/checklist/{checklist_id}`, `/api/v2/task/{task_id}/checklist`
- **Shared Hierarchy** — `/api/v2/team/{team_id}/shared`
- **Groups (User Groups)** — `/api/v2/team/{team_id}/group`
- **Task Templates** — `/api/v2/team/{team_id}/taskTemplate`
- **Guests** — `/api/v2/task/{task_id}/guest/{guest_id}`, etc.
- **Roles** — `/api/v2/team/{team_id}/customroles`

---

### 📖 OpenAPI Specification

ClickUp provides an OpenAPI specification that can be used with various API tools and code generators that support the OpenAPI format (V2 Open API Specification). You can access it at [developer.clickup.com/docs/open-api-spec](https://developer.clickup.com/docs/open-api-spec) for the definitive, machine-readable list of all endpoints.

### Rate Limiting
ClickUp enforces 100 requests per minute per token; add a small delay between calls when looping over tasks.

For the full, authoritative reference, visit: **[developer.clickup.com/reference](https://developer.clickup.com/reference/gettasks)**

# Confluence Cloud REST API v2 - Page Endpoints

Based on the schema provided, here are the Page API endpoints:

## Page Operations

| Endpoint | HTTP Method | Description |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `/pages` | GET | "Returns all pages" with pagination support |
| `/pages` | POST | Create a new page |
| `/pages/{id}` | GET | Retrieve a specific page by ID |
| `/pages/{id}` | PATCH | Update an existing page |
| `/pages/{id}` | DELETE | Remove a page |
| `/pages/{id}/children` | GET | Retrieve child pages |
| `/pages/{id}/versions` | GET | Access page version history |
| `/pages/{id}/labels` | GET | Fetch labels associated with a page |
| `/pages/{id}/properties` | GET | Get page properties |
| `/pages/{id}/comments` | GET | Retrieve page comments |

## Key Parameters

The GET endpoints support:
- **Pagination**: `cursor` and `limit` parameters
- **Filtering**: Status filters (current, archived, trashed)
- **Sorting**: Customizable sort orders
- **Expansion**: Include related content via `include-*` parameters

**Authentication**: Basic Auth or OAuth 2.0 with "read:page:confluence" and "write:page:confluence" scopes

# Trello API Endpoint Paths and Methods

Based on the provided documentation, here are the main Trello REST API endpoint groups available:

## API Endpoint Categories

The Trello REST API organizes endpoints into the following resource groups:

- **Actions** - Activity tracking and event management
- **Applications** - App-related operations
- **Batch** - Bulk request processing
- **Boards** - Board management and configuration
- **Cards** - Card operations and manipulation
- **Checklists** - Checklist and check item management
- **CustomFields** - Custom field definitions and values
- **Emoji** - Emoji resource access
- **Enterprises** - Enterprise-level management
- **Labels** - Label management
- **Lists** - List operations
- **Members** - Member profile and access management
- **Notifications** - Notification preferences and retrieval
- **Organizations** - Workspace management
- **Plugins** - Power-Ups and plugin integration
- **Search** - Content search functionality
- **Tokens** - API token management
- **Webhooks** - Event subscription and webhook configuration

## Access Details

The API base URL is: `https://api.trello.com/1`

Authentication uses either API Key or API Token passed as query parameters. The documentation provides "comprehensive reference material for developers integrating with Trello's platform" through these standardized REST endpoints.

For complete method signatures and parameters, consult the official Trello REST API reference documentation.

Here is an overview of the Trello REST API endpoints across all major resource groups:

## Trello REST API Endpoints Overview

### API Base URL
All Trello API endpoints use the base URL: `https://api.trello.com/1/`

Trello provides a simple RESTful web API where each type of resource (e.g., a card, a board, or a member) has a URI that you can interact with.

### Resource Groups

Trello REST API includes endpoints for actions, boards, cards, checklists, labels, lists, members, notifications, organizations, search functionality, sessions, tokens, and webhooks — essentially covering all aspects of the Trello project management platform.

---

### 1. **Boards** (`/boards`)
The Boards API group includes extensive endpoints such as:
- `GET /boards/{id}` – Get a board
- `PUT /boards/{id}` – Update a board
- `DEL /boards/{id}` – Delete a board
- `GET /boards/{id}/{field}` – Get a specific field
- `GET /boards/{boardId}/actions` – Get board actions
- `GET /boards/{boardId}/boardStars` – Get board stars
- `GET /boards/{id}/checklists` – Get checklists on a board
- `GET /boards/{id}/cards` – Get cards on a board
- `GET /boards/{id}/cards/{filter}` – Get filtered cards
- `GET /boards/{id}/customFields` – Get custom fields
- `GET /boards/{id}/labels` – Get labels
- `POST /boards/{id}/labels` – Create a label
- `GET /boards/{id}/lists` – Get lists
- `POST /boards/{id}/lists` – Create a list
- `GET /boards/{id}/lists/{filter}` – Get filtered lists
- `GET /boards/{id}/members` – Get members
- `PUT /boards/{id}/members` – Update members
- `PUT /boards/{id}/members/{idMember}` – Update a specific member
- `DEL /boards/{id}/members/{idMember}` – Remove a member
- `GET /boards/{id}/memberships` – Get memberships
- `PUT /boards/{id}/memberships/{idMembership}` – Update membership
- `POST /boards/` – Create a new board
- And various preferences endpoints (`myPrefs/emailPosition`, `myPrefs/showSidebar`, etc.)

---

### 2. **Cards** (`/cards`)
Key endpoints include:
- `GET /cards/{id}` – Get a card
- `PUT /cards/{id}` – Update a card
- `DEL /cards/{id}` – Delete a card
- `POST /cards` – Create a new card
- `GET /cards/{id}/actions` – Get card actions
- `GET /cards/{id}/attachments` – Get card attachments
- `GET /cards/{id}/checklists` – Get card checklists
- `GET /cards/{id}/members` – Get card members
- `GET /cards/{id}/labels` – Get card labels

A Card is the most basic unit of information in Trello. Cards have a name, description, labels, members, and a set of historical actions that have been taken on the card, including any comments.

---

### 3. **Lists** (`/lists`)
- `GET /lists/{id}` – Get a list
- `PUT /lists/{id}` – Update a list
- `POST /lists` – Create a new list
- `GET /lists/{id}/cards` – Get cards in a list
- `GET /lists/{id}/actions` – Get list actions

A List is a collection of Cards. In the standard Trello interface, Lists are stacked horizontally and are ordered on a board.

---

### 4. **Members** (`/members`)
- `GET /members/{id}` – Get a member
- `GET /members/{id}/boards` – Get member's boards
- `GET /members/{id}/cards` – Get member's cards
- `GET /members/{id}/organizations` – Get member's organizations
- `GET /members/{id}/notifications` – Get notifications

You can make a GET request to the `1/members/{memberId}/boards` endpoint and list all of the boards associated with your user.

---

### 5. **Organizations/Workspaces** (`/organizations`)
- `GET /organizations/{id}` – Get an organization
- `PUT /organizations/{id}` – Update an organization
- `DEL /organizations/{id}` – Delete an organization
- `GET /organizations/{id}/boards` – Get organization boards
- `GET /organizations/{id}/members` – Get organization members

---

### 6. **Checklists** (`/checklists`)
- `GET /checklists/{id}` – Get a checklist
- `POST /checklists` – Create a checklist
- `PUT /checklists/{id}` – Update a checklist
- `DEL /checklists/{id}` – Delete a checklist
- `GET /checklists/{id}/checkItems` – Get check items
- `POST /checklists/{id}/checkItems` – Create a check item

---

### 7. **Labels** (`/labels`)
- `GET /labels/{id}` – Get a label
- `PUT /labels/{id}` – Update a label
- `DEL /labels/{id}` – Delete a label
- `POST /labels` – Create a label

Labels can be as simple as colors attached to a Card, or Labels can have names.

---

### 8. **Webhooks** (`/webhooks`)
The Webhooks group includes:
- `POST /webhooks/` – Create a Webhook
- `GET /webhooks/{id}` – Get a Webhook
- `PUT /webhooks/{id}` – Update a Webhook
- `DEL /webhooks/{id}` – Delete a Webhook
- `GET /webhooks/{id}/{field}` – Get a field on a Webhook

Webhooks allow developers to receive notifications when a model within Trello changes. These models can include members, cards, boards, or other entities that actions can apply to. When an event occurs involving the monitored model, a webhook is triggered, sending an HTTP POST request to a specified callback URL with details about the action and the updated model.

---

### 9. **Search** (`/search`)
- `GET /search` – Search across Trello
- `GET /search/members` – Search for members

The Trello API supports searching across various entities including actions, boards, cards, members, and organizations.

---

### 10. **Additional Resource Groups**
The full API also covers: **Actions**, **CustomFields**, **Emoji**, **Enterprises**, **Notifications**, **Plugins**, **Tokens**, and more.

---

### Authentication
Every request requires an **API Key** and **Token** passed as query parameters:
```
?key=APIKey&token=APIToken
```

Trello's Application Programming Interface (API) provides access to Trello's resources programmatically. You can use the API to retrieve information from your boards, cards, and members, or create, update, and delete content using a script or other programming language.

### Rate Limits
To help prevent strain on Trello's servers, the API imposes rate limits per API key for all issued tokens. If a request exceeds the limit, Trello will return a 429 error along with a message corresponding to which limit was exceeded.

### Official Documentation
The full Trello API documentation is available at https://developer.atlassian.com/cloud/trello.

All seven scope map files have been created and validated. Here is a summary of what was built:

---

## Summary

### Files Created

| File | Provider | Endpoints | Scopes |
|------|----------|-----------|--------|
| `/workspace/scope-maps/notion.json` | Notion | 26 | 6 |
| `/workspace/scope-maps/linear.json` | Linear | 45 | 10 |
| `/workspace/scope-maps/confluence.json` | Confluence | 46 | 15 |
| `/workspace/scope-maps/asana.json` | Asana | 98 | 16 |
| `/workspace/scope-maps/monday.json` | Monday.com | 57 | 19 |
| `/workspace/scope-maps/clickup.json` | ClickUp | 91 | 16 |
| `/workspace/scope-maps/trello.json` | Trello | 105 | 3 |

### Per-Provider Details

**1. Notion** (`api.notion.com/v1`) -- 26 endpoints, 6 scopes
- Covers: Databases (create, get, update, query), Pages (create, get, update, delete, move, markdown), Blocks (get, list children, append children, update, delete), Users (list, get, me), Search, Comments (list, get, create, update, delete)
- Scopes: `read_content`, `update_content`, `insert_content`, `read_user_information`, `read_comments`, `insert_comments`
- Notion uses capability-based permissions where each integration capability maps to what the API can do (read content, update content, insert content, etc.)

**2. Linear** (`api.linear.app/graphql`) -- 45 endpoints, 10 scopes
- All operations use `POST /graphql` since Linear is a GraphQL API. Each entry describes the specific GraphQL query or mutation in the description field.
- Covers: Issues (list, get, create, update, delete, archive), Projects (list, get, create, update, delete), Teams (list, get), Users (list, get, viewer/me), Comments (list, create, update, delete), Labels (list, create, update, delete), Cycles (list, get, create, update), Workflow states, Webhooks, Attachments, Organizations, Initiatives
- Scopes: `read`, `write`, `issues:create`, `comments:create`, `admin`, `timeSchedule:write`, `initiative:read`, `initiative:write`, `customer:read`, `customer:write`
- The `read` scope is always required; `write` covers general mutations; specialized scopes like `issues:create` and `comments:create` allow narrow create-only permissions

**3. Confluence** (`your-domain.atlassian.net/wiki/api/v2`) -- 46 endpoints, 15 scopes
- Covers: Spaces (list, get, create, update, delete, permissions, properties), Pages (list, get, create, update, delete, children, versions, labels, properties, comments), Blog posts (list, get, create, update, delete), Comments (list, get, create, update, delete), Attachments (list, get, upload, delete), Labels, Search (CQL), Users, Tasks
- Scopes: Uses the Atlassian classic OAuth 2.0 (3LO) scope model including `read:confluence-content.all`, `write:confluence-content`, `read:confluence-space.summary`, `write:confluence-space`, `read:confluence-props`, `write:confluence-props`, `read:confluence-user`, `search:confluence`, and more

**4. Asana** (`app.asana.com/api/1.0`) -- 98 endpoints, 16 scopes
- The most endpoint-rich mapping since Asana has a fine-grained REST API with many sub-resource endpoints
- Covers: Tasks (CRUD, subtasks, dependencies, dependents, stories/comments, projects, tags, followers, attachments), Projects (CRUD, tasks, sections, members, followers, custom fields), Workspaces (list, get, update, typeahead), Users (list, get, me), Teams (list, get, members), Tags (CRUD), Sections (CRUD, add task), Custom fields (CRUD, enum options), Portfolios (CRUD, items), Goals (CRUD, subgoals, relationships), Status updates, Stories, Attachments, Webhooks
- Scopes: Semantic scopes like `tasks.read`, `tasks.write`, `projects.read`, `projects.write`, `workspaces.read`, `goals.read`, `goals.write`, `portfolios.read`, `portfolios.write`, etc. (Asana itself uses a broad `default` scope, but these semantic scopes are defined for proxy-based policy enforcement)

**5. Monday.com** (`api.monday.com/v2`) -- 57 endpoints, 19 scopes
- All operations use `POST /v2` since Monday.com is a GraphQL API. Each entry describes the specific query or mutation.
- Covers: Boards (list, get, create, update, delete, archive, activity logs), Items (list, get, create, update, delete, archive, move, subitems), Columns (list, create, rename, update metadata, delete), Groups (list, create, update, delete, archive, duplicate), Updates/comments (list, create, edit, delete, like), Users (list, me), Workspaces (list, create, update, delete), Tags, Teams, Account info, Views, Assets, Files, Notifications, Webhooks, Docs
- Scopes: `me:read`, `boards:read`, `boards:write`, `workspaces:read`, `workspaces:write`, `users:read`, `users:write`, `account:read`, `notifications:write`, `tags:read`, `teams:read`, `teams:write`, `updates:read`, `updates:write`, `assets:read`, `webhooks:read`, `webhooks:write`, `docs:read`, `docs:write`

**6. ClickUp** (`api.clickup.com/api/v2`) -- 91 endpoints, 16 scopes
- Follows ClickUp's hierarchy: Workspace -> Spaces -> Folders -> Lists -> Tasks
- Covers: Tasks (list, get, create, update, delete, filtered team tasks, multi-list), Lists (list in folder, folderless, get, create, update, delete), Folders (list, get, create, update, delete), Spaces (list, get, create, update, delete), Teams/workspaces (list), Goals (list, get, create, update, delete, key results), Comments (on tasks, lists, chat views; get, create, update, delete), Time tracking (entries, timers, legacy), Tags (list, create, update, delete, add/remove from task), Custom fields, Views (workspace/space/folder/list level), Attachments, Members, Dependencies, Checklists (items), Webhooks, User groups, Custom roles
- Scopes: `tasks.read`, `tasks.write`, `spaces.read`, `spaces.write`, `lists.read`, `lists.write`, `folders.read`, `folders.write`, `teams.read`, `goals.read`, `goals.write`, `comments.read`, `comments.write`, `time_tracking.read`, `time_tracking.write`, `webhooks.write`

**7. Trello** (`api.trello.com/1`) -- 105 endpoints, 3 scopes
- The most endpoint-rich but simplest scope model (just `read`, `write`, `account`)
- Covers: Boards (list, get, create, update, delete, lists, cards, members, memberships, labels, checklists, custom fields, actions, stars), Lists (get, create, update, archive, move, cards, actions, archive all cards, move all cards), Cards (get, create, update, delete, actions, comments, attachments, checklists, check items, labels, members, stickers, custom fields), Members (get, boards, cards, organizations, notifications), Organizations (list, get, create, update, delete, boards, members), Labels (CRUD), Checklists (CRUD, check items), Webhooks (CRUD), Search (general, members), Notifications, Custom fields (CRUD, options), Actions, Tokens, Batch
- Scopes: `read` (read-only access), `write` (read and write access), `account` (access to member email addresses)
